author: Jian-Hong Pan
message:
Merge pull request #640 from ramcq/claude/rebase-kernel-sva-patches-EPDPb

iommu/sva: Cherry-pick upstream patches to reinstate SVA on x86

author: Lu Baolu
message:
iommu/sva: invalidate stale IOTLB entries for kernel address space

Introduce a new IOMMU interface to flush IOTLB paging cache entries for
the CPU kernel address space.  This interface is invoked from the x86
architecture code that manages combined user and kernel page tables,
specifically before any kernel page table page is freed and reused.

This addresses the main issue with vfree() which is a common occurrence
and can be triggered by unprivileged users.  While this resolves the
primary problem, it doesn't address some extremely rare case related to
memory unplug of memory that was present as reserved memory at boot, which
cannot be triggered by unprivileged users.  The discussion can be found at
the link below.

Enable SVA on x86 architecture since the IOMMU can now receive
notification to flush the paging cache before freeing the CPU kernel page
table pages.

Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20251022082635.2462433-9-baolu.lu@linux.intel.com
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/linux-iommu/04983c62-3b1d-40d4-93ae-34ca04b827e5@intel.com/

author: Dave Hansen
message:
mm: introduce deferred freeing for kernel page tables

This introduces a conditional asynchronous mechanism, enabled by
CONFIG_ASYNC_KERNEL_PGTABLE_FREE.  When enabled, this mechanism defers the
freeing of pages that are used as page tables for kernel address mappings.
These pages are now queued to a work struct instead of being freed
immediately.

This deferred freeing allows for batch-freeing of page tables, providing a
safe context for performing a single expensive operation (TLB flush) for a
batch of kernel page tables instead of performing that expensive operation
for each page table.

Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20251022082635.2462433-8-baolu.lu@linux.intel.com

author: Lu Baolu
message:
x86/mm: use pagetable_free()

The kernel's memory management subsystem provides a dedicated interface,
pagetable_free(), for freeing page table pages.  Updates two call sites to
use pagetable_free() instead of the lower-level __free_page() or
free_pages().  This improves code consistency and clarity, and ensures the
correct freeing mechanism is used.

author: Dave Hansen
message:
mm: introduce pure page table freeing function

The pages used for ptdescs are currently freed back to the allocator in a
single location.  They will shortly be freed from a second location.

Create a simple helper that just frees them back to the allocator.

author: Dave Hansen
message:
x86/mm: use 'ptdesc' when freeing PMD pages

There are a billion ways to refer to a physical memory address.  One of
the x86 PMD freeing code location chooses to use a 'pte_t *' to point to a
PMD page and then call a PTE-specific freeing function for it.  That's a
bit wonky.

Just use a 'struct ptdesc *' instead.  Its entire purpose is to refer to
page table pages.  It also means being able to remove an explicit cast.

Right now, pte_free_kernel() is a one-liner that calls
pagetable_dtor_free().  Effectively, all this patch does is remove one
superfluous __pa(__va(paddr)) conversion and then call
pagetable_dtor_free() directly instead of through a helper.

Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20251022082635.2462433-5-baolu.lu@linux.intel.com

author: Dave Hansen
message:
mm: actually mark kernel page table pages

Now that the API is in place, mark kernel page table pages just after they
are allocated.  Unmark them just before they are freed.

Note: Unconditionally clearing the 'kernel' marking (via
ptdesc_clear_kernel()) would be functionally identical to what is here.
But having the if() makes it logically clear that this function can be
used for kernel and non-kernel page tables.

Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20251022082635.2462433-4-baolu.lu@linux.intel.com

author: Dave Hansen
message:
mm: add a ptdesc flag to mark kernel page tables

The page tables used to map the kernel and userspace often have very
different handling rules.  There are frequently *_kernel() variants of
functions just for kernel page tables.  That's not great and has lead to
code duplication.

Instead of having completely separate call paths, allow a 'ptdesc' to be
marked as being for kernel mappings.  Introduce helpers to set and clear
this status.

Note: this uses the PG_referenced bit.  Page flags are a great fit for
this since it is truly a single bit of information.  Use PG_referenced
itself because it's a fairly benign flag (as opposed to things like
PG_lock).  It's also (according to Willy) unlikely to go away any time
soon.

PG_referenced is not in PAGE_FLAGS_CHECK_AT_FREE.  It does not need to be
cleared before freeing the page, and pages coming out of the allocator
should have it cleared.  Regardless, introduce an API to clear it anyway.
Having symmetry in the API makes it easier to change the underlying
implementation later, like if there was a need to move to a
PAGE_FLAGS_CHECK_AT_FREE bit.

Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20251022082635.2462433-3-baolu.lu@linux.intel.com
Signed-off-by: Dave Hansen <dave.hansen@linux.intel.com>

author: Jian-Hong Pan
message:
Merge pull request #638 from endlessm/fix-rtl8852be-suspend

wifi: rtw89: 8852be: disable PCI D3Cold by quirk for certain platform

author: Jian-Hong Pan
message:
wifi: rtw89: 8852be: disable PCI D3Cold by quirk for ECS & Endless SE40AN

The RTL8852BE blocks system resume from suspend (s2idle) for a long
time, more than 70 seconds. And, the RTL8852BE wireless becomes
unavailable with D3cold power state. Notice kernel shows error messages:

[   76.323245] ACPI: EC: interrupt unblocked
[   77.744597] pcieport 0000:00:1c.0: broken device, retraining non-functional downstream link at 2.5GT/s
[   78.902711] pcieport 0000:00:1c.0: broken device, retraining non-functional downstream link at 2.5GT/s
[   79.990706] rtw89_8852be 0000:01:00.0: not ready 1023ms after resume; waiting
[   81.078661] rtw89_8852be 0000:01:00.0: not ready 2047ms after resume; waiting
[   83.190687] rtw89_8852be 0000:01:00.0: not ready 4095ms after resume; waiting
[   87.734697] rtw89_8852be 0000:01:00.0: not ready 8191ms after resume; waiting
[   96.438768] rtw89_8852be 0000:01:00.0: not ready 16383ms after resume; waiting
[  113.334715] rtw89_8852be 0000:01:00.0: not ready 32767ms after resume; waiting
[  147.638697] rtw89_8852be 0000:01:00.0: not ready 65535ms after resume; giving up
[  147.705135] rtw89_8852be 0000:01:00.0: failed to write DBI register, addr=0xB48
[  147.705376] rtw89_8852be 0000:01:00.0: failed to write DBI register, addr=0xB48
[  147.705378] rtw89_8852be 0000:01:00.0: failed to read PCI cap, ret=134
[  147.777603] rtw89_8852be 0000:01:00.0: xtal si not ready(W): offset=90 val=10 mask=10
[  147.798612] rtw89_8852be 0000:01:00.0: mac preinit fail, ret: -110

This patch follows "wifi: rtw89: 8852be: disable PCI D3Cold by quirk for
certain platform" [1] to disallow RTL8852BE's D3cold on ECS & Endless
SE40AN laptops to solve the resume from suspend issue.

[1]: https://patchwork.kernel.org/project/linux-wireless/patch/20250812004326.8582-1-pkshih@realtek.com/

BugLink: https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=221213
Signed-off-by: Jian-Hong Pan <jhp@endlessaccess.org>

author: Ping-Ke Shih
message:
wifi: rtw89: 8852be: disable PCI D3Cold by quirk for certain platform

On a platform, RTL8852BE gets lost from suspending, and kernel throws

  Unable to change power state from D3cold to D0, device inaccessible

then driver throws

  0000:39:00.0: failed to write DBI register, addr=0xB48
  0000:39:00.0: failed to write DBI register, addr=0xB48
  0000:39:00.0: failed to read PCI cap, ret=134
  0000:39:00.0: xtal si not ready(W): offset=90 val=10 mask=10
  0000:39:00.0: mac init fail, ret:-110

An solution is to disable d3cold via sysfs to write 1 to

  /sys/bus/pci/devices/0000:39:00.0/d3cold_allowed

Since it's hard to measure hardware signals to find cause, add a quirk to
disable d3cold for the platform.

Reported-by: Marcin Wojcik <wojcikmarcin49@gmail.com>
Closes: https://lore.kernel.org/linux-wireless/CAE3x6Qi8Nku26B4aWE+jtJmHb=hO8C743_myNrrBzUAGyuUw9A@mail.gmail.com/
Closes: https://github.com/morrownr/rtw89/issues/18
Signed-off-by: Ping-Ke Shih <pkshih@realtek.com>

author: Daniel Drake
message:
PCI: Disable AER for Skylake/Kabylake systems with Realtek wifi

Realtek cards on Skylake/Kabylake systems cause a huge amount of PCIe
AER error spam, so much that the mouse can't even be moved.

     pcieport 0000:00:1c.5: PCIe Bus Error: severity=Corrected, type=Physical Layer, id=00e5(Receiver ID)
     pcieport 0000:00:1c.5:   device [8086:9d15] error status/mask=00000001/00002000
     pcieport 0000:00:1c.5:    [ 0] Receiver Error
     pcieport 0000:00:1c.5: AER: Corrected error received: id=00e5
     pcieport 0000:00:1c.5: can't find device of ID00e5
     pcieport 0000:00:1c.5: AER: Corrected error received: id=00e5
     pcieport 0000:00:1c.5: can't find device of ID00e5

We think this is a pcieport bug - it can't find the device so it
doesn't clear the error condition - but for now we just add a workaround.

This workaround is intentionally restricted to SKL/KBL systems where the
realtek hardware is present behind the quirked PCI bridge, in order to
avoid side effects on other systems.

https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T12766
https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T15946
https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T16032
https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T17655
https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T18688
https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T34992

author: Jian-Hong Pan
message:
rtw88: Disable lps_deep_mode and ASPM for RTL8821CE

Realtek RTL8821CE wireless keeps failed from time to time. All clues
point to power management things between rtw88 and the firmware. We do
not have a better solution currently. So, disable the lps_deep_mode and
ASPM for RTL8821CE as a workaround now.

Note: RTL8821CE is the chip on one of Endless laptop: Asus X515EA

https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T33794

author: Jian-Hong Pan
message:
[Endless] packaging: Drop Rust packages from Build-Depends

Follows commit ("[Endless] config: Disable Rust").

Drop Rust packages from Build-Depends:
* rustc
* rust-src
* rustfmt
* bindgen

https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T34954

author: Jian-Hong Pan
message:
[Endless] config: Disable Rust

The Rust language is a new feature in Linux kernel. However, current EOS
does not have compatible Rust version. Besides, EOS does not use Rust in
kernel right now. So, disable CONFIG_RUST.

https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T34954

author: Derek Foreman
message:
[Endless] endlesspayg: Block ntpd's hwclock synching on payg systems

When PAYG is active, we don't want ntpd to be able to trigger hwclock
updates.

https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T30571

author: Derek Foreman
message:
[Endless] endlesspayg: Block RTC access

Stop direct RTC manipulation by tools such as hwclock. NTP's synched mode
can still cause 11m updates.

https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T30571

author: Matthew Leeds
message:
[Endless] endlesspayg: Deactivate LSM after permanent unlock

After a PAYG computer is permanently unlocked, the EOSPAYG_active EFI
variable is deleted. With that variable gone, on the next boot eos-paygd
will no longer activate the LSM, so it makes sense for the LSM to
deactivate itself; otherwise some other process could theoretically
activate it. See the ticket for more discussion.

https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T30284

author: Derek Foreman
message:
[Endless] endlesspayg: Protect SecureBootOption EFI variable

Some incredibly misguided EFI implementations have a vendor specific
SecureBootOption EFI variable that allows disabling secure boot
from userspace even when not in setup mode.

Block access to that for payg systems.

https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T27913

author: Derek Foreman
message:
[Endless] endlesspayg: Block proc populating for LSM user

Stop the pid directory in proc from being populated for the holder
of the LSM

https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T27044

author: Derek Foreman
message:
[Endless] endlesspayg: Block signals

This prevents signal delivery to the payg LSM owner. But, allows paygd
to receive SIGTERM so it can properly cleanup during shutdown.

https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T27663
https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T32959
https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T27663
https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T33088
https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T32815

author: Derek Foreman
message:
[Endless] watchdog: Endless software watchdog support

Add a simple software watchdog timer.

This watchdog uses a timeout of 20 minutes, per reasons detailed on
https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T27051#750436,
https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T27051#750443 and
https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T27051#751803

author: Daniel Drake
message:
[Endless] arm64 defconfig: Add options to enable RPi4 build

Similar to our changes to x86_64_defconfig, add some option
defaults that let you build a kernel image that is easy to boot
when doing kernel dev.

author: Daniel Drake
message: [Endless] x86_64 defconfig: add development options

author: João Paulo Rechi Vita
message:
[Endless] config: Include EOS Driver Signing 2025 certificate

Endless OS has EOS Driver Signing 2020 certificate to verify out-of-tree
modules under Secure Boot.  https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T30607

However, the Endless Secure Boot CA certificate was reissued to extend
the expiration date, and that causes the existing EOS Driver Signing
2020 certificate to appear invalid since it was issued before the CA
certificate.

This includes the new EOS Driver Signing 2025 certificate as part of
CONFIG_SYSTEM_TRUSTED_KEYS, so it can be used to verify out-of-tree
modules under Secure Boot.

This certificate is different than the one used to sign the kernel to
prevent PAYG systems from executing EOS kernels and breaking out of the
PAYG security domain. It will be used to sign out of tree modules such
as the nvidia driver.

```
$ openssl x509 -noout -subject -issuer -dates -in certs/endless.pem
subject=CN = EOS Driver Signing 2025
issuer=CN = Endless Secure Boot CA
notBefore=Feb  5 22:30:31 2025 GMT
notAfter=Feb  4 22:30:31 2030 GMT
```

Note that once this is merged any out of tree modules should be resigned
so that they're signatures match the certificate embedded here.

https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T33537
https://phabricator.endlessm.com/T35751
